message:
feat(layout): add TierUltra and TierMega for ultra-wide display layouts

Extend the layout tier system to support modern ultra-wide and mega-wide
displays with 3-panel and 5-panel dashboard layouts respectively.

Layout Tier Changes (internal/tui/layout/layout.go):
- Add TierUltra (240-319 cols) for 3-panel layouts with 25/50/25 proportions
- Add TierMega (320+ cols) for 5-panel cockpit layouts with 18/28/20/17/17 proportions
- Add MegaWideViewThreshold constant at 320 columns
- Implement UltraProportions() returning left/center/right widths with 6-col padding budget
- Implement MegaProportions() returning 5-panel widths with 10-col padding budget
- Update TierForWidth() to handle new tier thresholds

Dashboard Composition (internal/tui/dashboard/dashboard.go):
- Refactor View() to use switch statement for tier-based layout selection
- Add renderUltraLayout() composing Agents | Detail | Sidebar panels horizontally
- Add renderMegaLayout() composing 5 panels: Agents | Detail | Beads | Alerts | Activity
- Add renderSidebar() showing Activity & Locks with file reservation info and scan status
- Add placeholder renderBeadsPanel() and renderAlertsPanel() for future implementation
- Import sort package for future sorted displays

Mini-Bar Refactoring (internal/tui/dashboard/layout.go):
- Refactor RenderMiniBar() to use styles.MiniBar with explicit MiniBarPalette
- Define 4-tier color bands: Low (green), Mid (blue), MidHigh (yellow), High (red)
- Align threshold semantics with styles.MiniBar for consistency

Panel Interface (internal/tui/dashboard/panels/panel.go):
- Define Panel interface with Init, Update, View, Title, Priority, Keybindings methods
- Create PanelConfig struct for shared panel configuration
- Mirrors Bubble Tea lifecycle for composable panel architecture

Test Coverage:
- Add layout_test.go with TestTierForWidth covering all tier boundaries
- Add TestUltraProportions and TestMegaProportions validating width calculations
- Add dashboard_layout_test.go with TestPaneRowSelectionStyling_NoWrapAcrossWidths
- Add TestSplitViewLayouts_ByWidthTiers verifying split view composition

This is foundational work for the Dashboard 360-View Cockpit enhancement
(ntm-jpp1), enabling information-dense layouts on wide displays while
maintaining responsive behavior on narrower terminals.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ntm-2e9","title":"Implement stdin and --file support for ntm send","description":"Enable sending prompts from files and stdin pipes.

## What This Task Does

Extend ntm send to accept prompt content from:
1. --file flag: Read prompt from a file
2. stdin: When piped/redirected and no prompt argument given

## Use Cases

From file:
$ ntm send myproject --all --file prompts/review.md

From stdin (pipe):
$ cat error.log | ntm send myproject --cc 'Analyze this error:'
$ git diff | ntm send myproject --all 'Review these changes'

From stdin (redirect):
$ ntm send myproject --all < prompt.txt

Combined with prefix:
$ cat code.py | ntm send myproject --cc --prefix 'Review this code:\n'

## Technical Details

Detection logic:
1. If --file specified, read from file
2. Else if stdin is not a TTY, read from stdin
3. Else use positional prompt argument

Implementation:
func getPromptContent(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) (string, error) {
    if file != "" {
        content, err := os.ReadFile(file)
        return string(content), err
    }
    
    if !isatty.IsTerminal(os.Stdin.Fd()) {
        content, err := io.ReadAll(os.Stdin)
        return string(content), err
    }
    
    if len(args) > 1 {
        return args[1], nil
    }
    
    return "", errors.New("no prompt provided")
}

New flags:
--file, -f    Read prompt from file
--prefix      Text to prepend to file/stdin content
--suffix      Text to append to file/stdin content

## Edge Cases

- Empty file: Error with helpful message
- Binary file: Warning, but proceed if forced
- Very large file: Stream rather than load all into memory? (future)
- Encoding: Assume UTF-8

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --file reads prompt from specified path
- [ ] Stdin detected and read when not TTY
- [ ] --prefix and --suffix work with file/stdin
- [ ] Clear error messages for missing file, empty content
- [ ] Existing behavior unchanged when neither used

 {"id":"ntm-2th5","title":"Phase 4: Multi-Provider Support (Codex, Gemini)","description":"# Phase 4: Multi-Provider Support

## What This Is
Extend rotation support from Claude-only to Codex and Gemini.

## Why This is Phase 4 (Not Earlier)
- Claude is the primary use case for the user
- Get Claude working perfectly first
- Codex and Gemini can be added incrementally

## Provider Differences

### Claude Code
- Login: `/login` command
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth patterns: "successfully logged in", "authenticated as"
- Continuation: "continue. Use ultrathink"

### Codex CLI
- Login: `/logout` then restart (no in-session re-auth!)
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth: Happens on startup
- **Key difference**: Codex ALWAYS uses restart strategy

### Gemini CLI
- Login: `/auth` command
- Exit: `/exit` or Ctrl+C
- Auth patterns: TBD (need to research)
- Continuation: TBD

## Implementation

### Provider Interface
```go
type Provider interface {
    Name() string
    LoginCommand() string
    ExitCommand() string
    AuthSuccessPatterns() []string
    ContinuationPrompt() string
    SupportsReauth() bool  // Codex returns false
}

var providers = map[string]Provider{
    "claude": &ClaudeProvider{},
    "codex":  &CodexProvider{},
    "gemini": &GeminiProvider{},
}
```

### Codex Specifics
```go
type CodexProvider struct{}

func (p *CodexProvider) SupportsReauth() bool {
    return false // Codex must always restart
}

func (p *CodexProvider) LoginCommand() string {
    return "/logout" // Then quit and restart
}
```

## Research Needed
- [ ] Gemini CLI auth flow
- [ ] Gemini CLI auth success patterns
- [ ] Gemini continuation prompt (if any)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `ntm rotate --pane=N` works for Codex panes
- [ ] `ntm rotate --pane=N` works for Gemini panes
- [ ] Provider auto-detected from pane title","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:15:59.839364-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:11:57.906744-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:11:57.906744-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-2th5","depends_on_id":"ntm-qisk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:18:02.100867-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-2th5","depends_on_id":"ntm-t7ol","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:18:02.722254-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-2th5","depends_on_id":"ntm-z3uu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:19:26.950863-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-2th5","depends_on_id":"ntm-9f8k","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:19:27.36387-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-2th5","depends_on_id":"ntm-cs4z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T15:35:20.866608-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-3n9","title":"Implement agent type detection from pane content","description":"Improve agent type detection beyond pane title parsing.

## Current State
detectAgentType() in robot.go only checks pane title. This is unreliable because:
- Pane titles may not be set
- Users may use custom titles
- Agent type may not be in title

## Improved Detection
1. **Pane Title**: Check for 'claude', 'codex', 'gemini', etc.
2. **Process Name**: Check running process (claude, codex-cli, etc.)
3. **Pane Content**: Look for characteristic prompts/output:
   - Claude: '>' prompt, 'Claude' mentions
   - Codex: 'codex>' prompt, OpenAI references
   - Gemini: Gemini-specific formatting
   - Cursor: Cursor AI patterns
   - Windsurf: Windsurf patterns
   - Aider: 'aider>' prompt
4. **Environment**: Check for agent-specific env vars

## Detection Priority
Process name > Pane content > Pane title > Unknown

## Caching
Cache detected type per pane to avoid repeated content parsing. Invalidate on session reload.

## Output
```go
type AgentDetection struct {
    Type       string  // claude, codex, etc.
    Confidence float64 // 0.0-1.0
    Method     string  // how detected: process, content, title
}
```","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:53:43.118071-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:15.946452-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:26:15.946452-05:00","comments":[{"id":11,"issue_id":"ntm-3n9","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Detection Strategies

### Priority Order
1. **Process-based** (highest confidence)
   - ps aux | grep for process names: claude, codex-cli, gemini-cli
   - Check /proc/{pid}/cmdline on Linux
   - Most reliable but requires shell execution

2. **Environment-based**
   - Check for CLAUDE_*, OPENAI_*, GOOGLE_* env vars in pane
   - Can infer agent type from env configuration

3. **Content-based**
   - Scan last N lines of pane output
   - Look for characteristic patterns:
     - Claude: '>' prompt, 'Claude' in output, tool use XML
     - Codex: 'codex>' prompt, OpenAI references
     - Gemini: 'gemini>' prompt
     - Aider: 'aider>' or '/help' in prompt
   
4. **Title-based** (lowest confidence)
   - Current implementation - fallback only

### Caching Strategy
```go
type AgentTypeCache struct {
    cache map[string]CachedDetection // key: session:pane
    ttl   time.Duration              // default 60s
    mu    sync.RWMutex
}

type CachedDetection struct {
    Type       string
    Confidence float64
    Method     string
    DetectedAt time.Time
}
```

### Confidence Scores
- Process match: 0.95
- Env match: 0.85
- Content match: 0.70
- Title match: 0.50
- Unknown: 0.0","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:55:48Z"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-5zc","title":"EPIC: Multi-Model Support","description":"# Multi-Model Support

## Background & Motivation

Currently, NTM treats agent types as monolithic:
- `--cc` spawns Claude with a fixed command
- `--cod` spawns Codex with a fixed command
- `--gmi` spawns Gemini with a fixed command

But in practice, power users need model flexibility:
- Use Claude Opus for architecture decisions (expensive, powerful)
- Use Claude Sonnet for implementation (cheaper, fast)
- Use GPT-4 for one task, o1 for reasoning-heavy tasks
- Mix Gemini Pro and Flash based on complexity

## The Problem

Real-world multi-agent workflows look like:
```
Architect (Opus) → Designer (Sonnet) → Implementer (Codex) → Reviewer (Opus)
```

NTM should enable this naturally without manual agent configuration.

## Proposed Syntax

### Spawn with model specifiers
```bash
# Basic: 2 Opus, 1 Sonnet
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2:opus --cc=1:sonnet

# Full model names
ntm spawn myproject --cc=1:claude-opus-4 --cc=2:claude-sonnet-4

# Codex variants
ntm spawn myproject --cod=1:gpt4 --cod=1:o1

# Gemini variants
ntm spawn myproject --gmi=1:pro --gmi=1:flash
```

### Add with model specifiers
```bash
ntm add myproject --cc=1:haiku  # Add cheap agent for simple tasks
```

## Model Aliases

Short aliases mapped to full model names in config:
```toml
[models.claude]
opus = "claude-opus-4"
sonnet = "claude-sonnet-4"
haiku = "claude-haiku"

[models.codex]
gpt4 = "gpt-4"
o1 = "o1"

[models.gemini]
pro = "gemini-pro"
flash = "gemini-flash"
```

## Pane Naming Enhancement

Current: `myproject__cc_1`
Enhanced: `myproject__cc_opus_1` or `myproject__cc_1_opus`

This enables filtering by model:
```bash
ntm send myproject --cc:opus "Architecture review needed"
ntm send myproject --cc:sonnet "Implement the design"
```

## Dashboard Display

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Panes                                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● cc_1   Claude Opus    working                  │
│ ● cc_2   Claude Sonnet  idle                     │
│ ● cod_1  GPT-4          working                  │
│ ● cod_2  o1             thinking                 │
│ ● gmi_1  Gemini Pro     idle                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] spawn accepts model specifiers (--cc=N:model)
- [ ] Model aliases configurable in config.toml
- [ ] Pane titles include model for identification
- [ ] send command can filter by model
- [ ] Status and dashboard show model info
- [ ] Robot mode includes model in agent output

## Implementation Notes

- Backwards compatible: `--cc=2` uses default model
- Model specifier parsed: count:model format
- Agent command template expanded with model variable","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:35.225729-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T16:17:37.840476-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T16:17:37.840476-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-5zs","title":"Create SSH wrapper for tmux commands","description":"Enable tmux commands to execute on remote hosts via SSH.

## What This Task Does

Create an abstraction layer that allows all tmux operations to run
either locally or on a remote host via SSH.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --ssh devbox --cc=4
$ ntm list --ssh devbox
$ ntm attach myproject --ssh devbox

## Technical Approach

Modify internal/tmux package to support remote execution:

type TmuxClient struct {
    Remote string // empty for local, "user@host" for remote
}

func (c *TmuxClient) Run(args ...string) (string, error) {
    if c.Remote == "" {
        return exec.Command("tmux", args...).Output()
    }
    sshArgs := append([]string{c.Remote, "tmux"}, args...)
    return exec.Command("ssh", sshArgs...).Output()
}

## SSH Configuration

Support SSH config hosts:
- $ ntm list --ssh devbox   # Uses ~/.ssh/config host
- $ ntm list --ssh user@192.168.1.100

Respect SSH options:
- ControlMaster for connection reuse
- Identity files
- Proxy jumps

## Error Handling

Connection failures:
- Clear error message with SSH exit code
- Suggest checking SSH connectivity
- Timeout handling

Auth failures:
- Detect auth errors
- Suggest ssh-add for key issues

## Session Context

Store remote host in tmux environment variable:
$ tmux set-environment -t myproject NTM_REMOTE_HOST devbox

When operating on existing session, detect remote host:
- If --ssh provided, use it
- Else check NTM_REMOTE_HOST
- Else assume local

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] TmuxClient abstraction supports local and remote
- [ ] All existing tmux functions work with remote
- [ ] SSH config hosts supported
- [ ] Connection errors handled gracefully
- [ ] Remote host stored with session

 {"id":"ntm-61i","title":"EPIC: Agent Organization & Filtering","description":"Enable logical grouping and filtering of agents via tags.

## Problem Statement

Current agent organization is limited to sessions:
- All agents in a session are treated as one group
- Can't target subsets like 'all frontend agents' across sessions
- Can't filter ntm list to show only certain agent types
- No way to apply operations to logical groups

As agent counts grow, this becomes unwieldy. Users run 10+ agents across
multiple sessions and need better organization primitives.

## Goals

1. Add tagging system for agents (multiple tags per agent)
2. Filter all operations by tag (send, list, kill, status)
3. Tags persist across restarts (stored in pane title metadata)
4. Support tag expressions (AND, OR, NOT)

## Why This Matters

Tags provide a flexible, user-defined organization layer:
- Group by concern: frontend, backend, database, tests
- Group by purpose: refactor, bugfix, feature, research
- Group by priority: critical, background, experimental
- Group by lifecycle: temporary, persistent

This enables workflows like:
- 'Send status update to all backend agents'
- 'Kill all experimental agents'
- 'List only agents working on the auth feature'

## Success Criteria

- ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --tags=frontend,ui
- ntm add myproject --cod=1 --tags=backend,api
- ntm send --tag=frontend 'Update the navbar'
- ntm list --tag=backend
- ntm kill --tag=experimental
- Tags visible in ntm status output

## Technical Approach

Tags stored in tmux pane title:
- Current format: session__cc_1
- New format: session__cc_1[frontend,ui]

Tag operations:
- Parse tags from pane title during list operations
- Match tags using simple OR logic (any match = included)
- Future: Support expressions like 'frontend AND NOT experimental'

Config for default tags:
[agents.claude]
default_tags = ["ai", "code"]

## Interaction with Other Features

- JSON Output: Tags included in --json output
- Notifications: Can filter notifications by tag
- Health Checks: Can check health of tagged subset
- Remote Sessions: Tags work across remote sessions

## Design Decisions

Why pane titles for storage?
- Already using pane titles for agent type info
- Survives session detach/reattach
- Visible in tmux list-panes
- No external state to sync

Why not a separate database?
- Adds complexity
- Requires syncing with tmux state
- Pane titles are the source of truth anyway

 {"id":"ntm-66c","title":"Create Test Harness with Structured Logging","description":"# Test Harness with Structured Logging

## Purpose
Foundational infrastructure for all integration and E2E tests.

## Components

### 1. Test Logger
```go
package testutil

type TestLogger struct {
    w        io.Writer
    testName string
    startTs  time.Time
}

func NewTestLogger(t *testing.T, logDir string) *TestLogger {
    filename := fmt.Sprintf("%s_%s.log", t.Name(), time.Now().Format("20060102_150405"))
    f, _ := os.Create(filepath.Join(logDir, filename))
    t.Cleanup(func() { f.Close() })
    return &TestLogger{w: f, testName: t.Name(), startTs: time.Now()}
}

func (l *TestLogger) Log(format string, args ...interface{}) {
    ts := time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339)
    fmt.Fprintf(l.w, "[%s] %s\n", ts, fmt.Sprintf(format, args...))
}

func (l *TestLogger) Exec(cmd string, args ...string) ([]byte, error) {
    l.Log("EXEC: %s %s", cmd, strings.Join(args, " "))
    out, err := exec.Command(cmd, args...).CombinedOutput()
    l.Log("OUTPUT: %s", string(out))
    l.Log("EXIT: %v", err)
    return out, err
}
```

### 2. Session Helper
```go
func CreateTestSession(t *testing.T, logger *TestLogger, agents map[string]int) string {
    name := fmt.Sprintf("ntm_test_%d", time.Now().UnixNano())
    args := []string{"spawn", name}
    for agentType, count := range agents {
        args = append(args, fmt.Sprintf("--%s=%d", agentType, count))
    }
    logger.Exec("ntm", args...)
    t.Cleanup(func() {
        logger.Log("TEARDOWN: Killing session %s", name)
        exec.Command("ntm", "kill", "-f", name).Run()
    })
    return name
}
```

### 3. Assertion Helpers
```go
func AssertSessionExists(t *testing.T, logger *TestLogger, name string) {
    logger.Log("VERIFY: Session %s exists", name)
    err := exec.Command("tmux", "has-session", "-t", name).Run()
    if err != nil {
        logger.Log("FAIL: Session does not exist")
        t.Errorf("Session %s should exist", name)
    } else {
        logger.Log("PASS: Session exists")
    }
}
```

### 4. Skip Conditions
```go
func RequireTmux(t *testing.T) {
    if _, err := exec.LookPath("tmux"); err != nil {
        t.Skip("tmux not installed")
    }
}

func RequireNTMBinary(t *testing.T) {
    if _, err := exec.LookPath("ntm"); err != nil {
        t.Skip("ntm binary not in PATH")
    }
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-6pd","title":"EPIC: Scriptability & Automation Foundation","description":"Enable NTM to be scripted, piped, and composed with other Unix tools.

## Problem Statement

NTM is currently a human-interactive tool. All output is formatted for human reading, and input must come from command-line arguments. This severely limits:

1. **Scripting**: Can't parse ntm list output reliably in shell scripts
2. **CI/CD Integration**: No machine-readable output for automation pipelines  
3. **Composition**: Can't pipe NTM into jq, grep, awk, or other Unix tools
4. **Complex Prompts**: Can't send multi-line prompts, file contents, or piped data
5. **Reproducibility**: Hard to replay exact workflows programmatically

## Goals

1. Add --json flag to all commands producing structured, parseable output
2. Support stdin and --file for prompt input to ntm send
3. Establish consistent JSON schemas across all commands
4. Maintain human-friendly defaults while enabling machine mode

## Why This Matters

The Unix philosophy: tools should do one thing well and compose with others. NTM orchestrates agents, but without scriptability, users can't build on top of it.

Real-world scenarios this enables:
- ntm list --json | jq '.[] | select(.agents.claude > 0) | .name'
- ntm status myproject --json | jq '.panes[] | select(.status == "idle")'
- cat requirements.txt | ntm send myproject --all "Implement these features"
- git diff HEAD~5 | ntm send myproject --cc "Review these changes"
- CI pipelines that spawn agents, send prompts, and verify outputs

## Success Criteria

- Every command that produces output supports --json
- JSON schemas are documented and consistent
- ntm send accepts --file and reads from stdin when not a TTY
- Scripts can reliably parse NTM output without fragile text parsing
- Exit codes are meaningful (0=success, 1=error, 2=partial)

## Technical Considerations

- JSON output should include all data, not just what's shown in text mode
- Timestamps should be ISO 8601
- Nested structures where appropriate (e.g., panes within sessions)
- Schema versioning for future compatibility
- Streaming JSON (JSON lines) for long-running commands

## Relation to Existing Patterns

AGENTS.md shows this pattern with other tools:
- cass uses --robot and --json flags for machine output
- bd uses --json for structured issue data
- bv uses --robot-* commands for AI consumption

NTM should follow the same pattern for ecosystem consistency.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:24.530541-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:21:11.005784-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:21:11.005784-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-6tw","title":"EPIC: Context Window Intelligence","description":"# Context Window Intelligence

## Background & Motivation

AI coding agents have finite context windows. As agents work, their context fills up. When context approaches limits:
- Agent performance degrades
- Responses become confused or repetitive
- Critical context may be lost

**Problem**: Users have no visibility into context usage.

## CRITICAL: Honest About Limitations

**We cannot measure actual context usage.** The agent CLIs don't expose this data.

What we CAN do:
1. **Estimate** based on visible output (scrollback)
2. **Detect patterns** that suggest context exhaustion
3. **Show relative usage** (low/medium/high/critical)
4. **Warn** when estimates suggest problems

**What we show is an ESTIMATE, not actual usage.**

## Estimation Approach

### Method 1: Scrollback-Based Estimate
- Capture pane scrollback (visible conversation)
- Apply overhead multiplier (system prompts, tool calls, hidden context)
- This gives a rough LOWER BOUND on usage

### Method 2: Heuristic Detection
Detect behavioral patterns suggesting context exhaustion:
- Repeated identical tool calls
- "I apologize" / confusion patterns
- Forgetting recent context
- Circular reasoning

### Display Philosophy
Instead of precise percentages, use qualitative indicators:
```
cc_1 (opus)    [████░░░░░░] Low      (~50k est.)
cc_2 (sonnet)  [███████░░░] Medium   (~120k est.)
cod_1 (gpt4)   [█████████░] High     (~100k est.) ⚠️
gmi_1 (pro)    [██░░░░░░░░] Low      (~100k est.)
```

The "est." suffix reminds users these are estimates.

## Technical Details

### Token Estimation
Use character-based heuristic (~3.5 chars/token for English):
```go
func EstimateTokens(text string) int {
    return len(text) * 10 / 35  // ~3.5 chars per token
}
```

Simpler and more honest than pretending to have accurate tokenizers.

### Overhead Multiplier
Visible scrollback is only part of context. Apply 2-3x multiplier:
- System prompts: ~2-5k tokens
- Tool definitions: ~5-15k tokens
- Hidden conversation: varies

Conservative default: 2.5x multiplier

### Context Limits
```go
var ApproxContextLimits = map[string]int{
    "opus":   200000,
    "sonnet": 200000,
    "haiku":  200000,
    "gpt4":   128000,
    "o1":     128000,
    "pro":    1000000,
    "flash":  1000000,
}
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Scrollback-based estimation implemented
- [ ] Qualitative display (Low/Medium/High/Critical)
- [ ] Clear "estimate" labeling throughout
- [ ] Heuristic exhaustion detection (patterns)
- [ ] Configurable warning threshold
- [ ] Dashboard integration with context bars
- [ ] Honest about what we don't know","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:24:43.820362-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T16:25:56.372455-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T16:25:56.372455-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-84t","title":"Implement auto-restart for crashed agents","description":"Automatically restart agents that crash or exit unexpectedly.

## What This Task Does

Add --auto-restart flag to ntm spawn that monitors agents and restarts
them if they crash. This builds on the health check infrastructure.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --auto-restart
Starting agents with auto-restart enabled...
[Agent cc_1 crashed, restarting in 30s...]
[Agent cc_1 restarted successfully]

Configuration:
[resilience]
auto_restart = true
max_restarts = 3
restart_delay_seconds = 30

## Technical Approach

Option 1: Background goroutine
- Spawn launches a monitor goroutine
- Periodically checks health
- Restarts crashed agents
- Exits when session ends

Option 2: Separate daemon
- ntm spawn --auto-restart starts a background process
- Process monitors and restarts
- Survives terminal close

Recommendation: Start with Option 1 (simpler), evolve to Option 2 if needed.

## Restart Logic

1. Detect crash via health check (exit code != 0, process gone)
2. Check restart count for this agent
3. If under max_restarts:
   a. Wait restart_delay_seconds
   b. Get agent type from pane title
   c. Re-run agent command in same pane
   d. Increment restart count
4. If over max_restarts:
   a. Log failure
   b. Send notification (if enabled)
   c. Stop monitoring this agent

## State Tracking

Need to track per-agent:
- Restart count since session start
- Last restart time
- Original agent command

Store in:
- Memory (simple, lost on ntm exit)
- Tmux environment variable (persists)
- File (overkill for now)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --auto-restart flag enables monitoring
- [ ] Crashed agents are detected and restarted
- [ ] Restart delay is configurable
- [ ] Max restart limit is enforced
- [ ] Restart count shown in health output
- [ ] Works with existing spawn command

## Problem Statement
NTM is currently manual and reactive:
- Each prompt requires explicit user action
- No way to chain agent outputs as inputs to other agents
- No notifications when agents finish or error
- No integration with external tools (CI, Slack, etc.)
- File content must be manually copied into prompts

## Goals
1. Enable pipeline mode (chain agents sequentially)
2. Support webhook/notification on events
3. Easy file content injection into prompts
4. Token/cost estimation before sending expensive prompts

## Success Criteria
- `ntm pipeline` chains agent stages with output passing
- Config supports on_error, on_complete webhooks
- `ntm send --file=path` injects file content
- `ntm send --estimate` shows token/cost estimate

## Pipeline Mode
```bash
ntm pipeline myproject \
  --stage="cc: Design the authentication module" \
  --stage="cod: Implement the design from previous stage" \
  --stage="gmi: Write tests for the implementation"
```

Each stage:
1. Sends prompt to specified agent type
2. Waits for completion (detect idle state)
3. Captures output
4. Passes output as context to next stage

## Webhook Config
```toml
[notifications]
on_error = "curl -X POST https://slack.webhook/..."
on_complete = "terminal-notifier -message 'Done'"
on_rate_limit = "echo 'Rate limited' >> ~/ntm-alerts.log"
```

```bash

  --file=src/auth.py \
  --file=src/models.py:10-50 \
  "Refactor these files to use dependency injection"

Injects files with formatting:
```
# File: src/auth.py
\`\`\`python
<file contents>
\`\`\`
```

## Cost Estimation
- Count tokens in prompt using tiktoken or similar
- Estimate based on known pricing (Claude, Codex, Gemini)
- Show estimate, ask for confirmation on expensive prompts

- Sophisticated multi-stage workflows
- Stay informed without constant monitoring
- Faster prompt construction with file injection
- Budget awareness for API-heavy usage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:38:05.291885-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:25:11.37538-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:25:11.37538-05:00"}

## Extended Syntax

```

## Implementation
```go
type FileSpec struct {
    Path      string
    StartLine int  // 0 = from beginning
    EndLine   int  // 0 = to end
}

func ParseFileSpec(spec string) (FileSpec, error) {
    // Parse "path:start-end" format
}

func InjectFiles(specs []FileSpec, prompt string) (string, error) {
    var parts []string
    
    for _, spec := range specs {
        content, err := readFileRange(spec)
        if err != nil {
            return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to read %s: %w", spec.Path, err)
        }
        
        lang := detectLanguage(spec.Path)
        header := fmt.Sprintf("# File: %s", spec.Path)
        if spec.StartLine > 0 || spec.EndLine > 0 {
            header += fmt.Sprintf(" (lines %d-%d)", spec.StartLine, spec.EndLine)
        }
        
        parts = append(parts, fmt.Sprintf("%s\n```%s\n%s\n```", 
            header, lang, content))
    }
    
    parts = append(parts, "---\n\n"+prompt)
    return strings.Join(parts, "\n\n"), nil
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-cyq","title":"Implement token estimation library","description":"# Implement Token Estimation Library

## Purpose

Provide rough token estimates for context usage visualization.

## IMPORTANT: Estimation, Not Measurement

We're ESTIMATING tokens, not measuring actual usage. Be honest about this.

## Approach: Character-Based Heuristic

Simpler and more honest than pretending to have accurate tokenizers:

```go
package tokens

// EstimateTokens provides a rough token count estimate.
// Uses ~3.5 characters per token heuristic for English text.
// This is an ESTIMATE - actual token counts vary by model and content.
func EstimateTokens(text string) int {
    // ~3.5 chars per token for typical English
    return len(text) * 10 / 35
}

// EstimateWithOverhead applies overhead multiplier for hidden context.
// Overhead includes: system prompts, tool definitions, conversation structure.
func EstimateWithOverhead(visibleText string, multiplier float64) int {
    visible := EstimateTokens(visibleText)
    return int(float64(visible) * multiplier)
}
```

## Model Context Limits

Approximate limits (may change, conservative estimates):
```go
var ContextLimits = map[string]int{
    "opus":   200000,
    "sonnet": 200000,
    "haiku":  200000,
    "gpt4":   128000,
    "o1":     128000,
    "pro":    1000000,
    "flash":  1000000,
}

func GetContextLimit(model string) int {
    if limit, ok := ContextLimits[normalizeModel(model)]; ok {
        return limit
    }
    return 128000 // Conservative default
}
```

## Why Not Use tiktoken?

1. tiktoken is for OpenAI tokenizers - Claude uses different tokenization
2. Each model family has different tokenizers
3. Character heuristic is 80% accurate and works for ALL models
4. We're showing estimates anyway - precision is false confidence

## Problem Statement

AI coding agents are resource-intensive:
- High CPU usage during inference
- Significant memory for context windows
- Can slow down local development machines
- Laptop fans spinning constantly

Users want to run agents on powerful remote machines while keeping
their local environment responsive. Tmux already supports remote
sessions, but NTM doesn't expose this capability.

## Goals

1. Add --ssh flag to spawn, list, attach, send, status, kill
2. Seamlessly manage remote sessions as if they were local
3. Support SSH config hosts (~/.ssh/config)
4. Handle connection failures gracefully
5. Remember remote host association for sessions

## Why This Matters

Power users have access to:
- Cloud VMs with high CPU/memory
- Development servers with fast internet
- Dedicated machines for agent workloads
- GPU instances for local LLM agents

NTM should leverage these resources without requiring users to
manually SSH and run commands.

## Success Criteria

- ntm spawn myproject --ssh devbox --cc=4
- ntm list --ssh devbox
- ntm attach myproject --ssh devbox
- ntm send myproject --ssh devbox --all 'status'
- ntm status myproject --ssh devbox --json
- ntm kill myproject --ssh devbox

## Technical Approach

SSH wrapper for tmux commands:
- Local: tmux new-session -d -s myproject
- Remote: ssh devbox tmux new-session -d -s myproject

Connection management:
- Use user's SSH config and keys
- Support ControlMaster for connection reuse
- Timeout and retry on connection failures
- Clear error messages for auth failures

Session metadata:
- Store remote host with session (in tmux environment variable)
- Auto-detect remote host when operating on existing sessions
- Allow explicit override with --ssh flag

## Considerations

SSH Key Management:
- Rely on user's existing SSH setup
- Don't store passwords or implement auth
- Support ssh-agent for key passphrase caching

Network Reliability:
- Graceful handling of disconnects
- Retry logic for transient failures
- Clear feedback on connection status

Performance:
- Each command requires SSH connection (acceptable)
- Consider ControlMaster recommendation for users
- Batch operations where possible

## Interaction with Other Epics

- Session Persistence (ntm-bh5): Saved sessions include remote host
- Health Checks: Health monitoring works over SSH
- Notifications: Webhook notifications work regardless of locality
- Agent Tagging: Tags work the same locally and remotely

## Future Possibilities

- ntm ssh-setup: Help configure ControlMaster
- Connection pooling within ntm process
- Support for SSH jump hosts
- Parallel operations across multiple remotes

 {"id":"ntm-d2t","title":"Implement --robot-send for batch messaging","description":"Send prompts to multiple agents atomically with structured result.

## Rationale
Orchestrating N agents often means broadcasting instructions ("all agents: run tests") or targeted multi-cast ("agents 1,2: work on feature X while agent 3: does Y"). Current approach requires N separate send commands. This consolidates into one atomic operation.

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-send myproject --all --msg="run tests and report results"
ntm --robot-send myproject --panes=0.1,0.2 --msg="implement feature X"
ntm --robot-send myproject --type=claude --msg="claude-specific instruction"
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "sent_at": "ISO8601",
  "targets": ["0.1", "0.2", "1.0"],
  "successful": ["0.1", "0.2"],
  "failed": [{"pane": "1.0", "error": "pane not found"}],
  "message_preview": "run tests and..."
}
```

## Features
- --all: broadcast to all panes
- --panes=X,Y,Z: specific pane IDs
- --type=claude: filter by agent type
- --exclude=0.3: exclude specific panes
- --delay-ms=100: stagger sends to avoid thundering herd

## Atomicity
Either report all successes/failures - never partial state. If sending to 5 panes and 2 fail, I need to know which 2 and why.

## Integration with Agent Mail
Consider adding --via-mail flag that routes through agent-mail instead of direct tmux send. ```go
type CommandEntry struct {
    ID        uint64
    Session   string
    Pane      string
    Command   string
    SentAt    time.Time
    AckAt     *time.Time       // when agent acked
    OutputLen int              // lines of output produced
    Duration  *time.Duration   // if completed
}
```

## Query Interface
```bash
ntm --robot-history myproject                    # all history
ntm --robot-history myproject --pane=0.1         # specific pane

}
```

## Rationale
When an agent is working on a task, I need to understand progress:
- Is it just starting?
- Is it midway through?
- Is it finishing up?
- Has it encountered a blocker?

This enables smarter orchestration (don't interrupt agents near completion, reassign stuck work, etc.)

## Progress Indicators
Detect progress from pane output patterns:

### Starting Indicators
- 'Let me', 'I'll', 'Starting'
- File reads, searches
- Planning/analysis output

### Working Indicators
- File edits
- Code blocks being written
- Test runs
- Build commands

### Finishing Indicators
- 'Done', 'Complete', 'Finished'
- Summary output
- Return to prompt
- Commit messages

### Stuck Indicators
- 'Error', 'Failed', 'Cannot'
- Repeated attempts
- Questions asked
- Long pauses

## Output in Snapshot
```json
{
  "agents": [{
    "pane": "0.1",
    "progress": {
      "stage": "working",     // starting, working, finishing, stuck
      "confidence": 0.75,
      "indicators": ["file_edits", "code_blocks"],
      "time_in_stage_sec": 120
    }
  }]
}
```

## Future: ML-based Detection
Could train a classifier on agent outputs to detect progress more accurately. ## Rationale
When orchestrating N agents across M sessions working on P beads with Q mail threads, I currently need separate queries for each dimension. This compounds latency (4-6 tool calls) and burns tokens on redundant parsing. A unified snapshot gives me complete situational awareness in one atomic read.

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "ts": "ISO8601",
  "sessions": [{
    "name": "string",
    "agents": [{
      "pane": "window.pane",
      "type": "claude|codex|gemini|cursor|windsurf|aider",
      "state": "active|idle|stuck|waiting",
      "last_output_age_sec": number,
      "output_tail_lines": number,
      "current_bead": "bead-id|null",
      "pending_mail": number
    }]
  }],
  "beads_summary": {"open": N, "in_progress": N, "blocked": N, "ready": N},
  "mail_unread": number,
  "alerts": ["string"]
}
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Always include everything (no optional fields that require follow-up)
- Use compact field names (abbreviations OK for frequently-accessed data)
- Include computed fields (last_output_age_sec vs raw timestamp)
- Alerts are pre-filtered to actionable items only

+package panels
+
+import (
+	"github.com/charmbracelet/bubbles/key"
+	tea "github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea"
+)
+
+// Panel defines the common interface all dashboard panels must implement.
+// It intentionally mirrors the Bubble Tea model lifecycle so panels can be
+// composed inside the dashboard without bespoke glue code.
+type Panel interface {
+	// Init returns the initial command for the panel.
+	Init() tea.Cmd
+
+	// Update applies a message to the panel and returns the updated panel and
+	// an optional command to run.
+	Update(msg tea.Msg) (Panel, tea.Cmd)
+
+	// View renders the panel.
+	View() string
+
+	// Title is the human-readable panel name for headers and focus indicators.
+	Title() string
+
+	// Priority is used to order panels when space is constrained.
+	Priority() int
+
+	// Keybindings returns the key bindings the panel wants to expose to the
+	// dashboard (e.g., for focus or command palette help).
+	Keybindings() []key.Binding
+}
+
+// PanelConfig captures the shared configuration used when constructing a panel.
+// It allows caller-provided metadata (title/priority) and optional keybindings
+// to be applied consistently across panel implementations.
+type PanelConfig struct {
+	Title       string
+	Priority    int
+	Keybindings []key.Binding
+}
